_meta.authoringNotes: Numbers cross-checked against AAP Red Book 2024-2027 (32nd ed), CDC GBS Perinatal Disease Prevention 2019, NeoReviews 2020-2024 series, Cochrane neonatal antibiotic reviews, and Avery's Diseases of the Newborn 11th ed. Empiric regimens reflect AAP NRP 8th ed and 2018 AAP Committee on Fetus and Newborn statements.

h1Title: Congenital Pneumonia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Congenital pneumonia is a lung infection that develops in a newborn either before delivery, during passage through the birth canal, or within the first 48 to 72 hours of life. It complicates roughly 1 to 10 of every 1,000 live births in high-income settings and rates rise sharply with prematurity, prolonged rupture of membranes, and maternal chorioamnionitis. Affected infants typically present in the delivery room or within hours of birth with tachypnea above 60 breaths per minute, grunting, intercostal and subcostal retractions, nasal flaring, cyanosis, and temperature instability. Group B Streptococcus, Escherichia coli, and Listeria monocytogenes dominate early-onset disease, while TORCH organisms cause transplacental infection. Empiric ampicillin plus gentamicin started within the first hour of recognized illness, alongside respiratory support, drives a mortality difference between 5 percent in term infants and up to 50 percent in extremely preterm neonates.

keyStats.prevalence: 1-10 per 1,000 live births in high-income settings; 5-50 per 1,000 in low- and middle-income countries (WHO 2022)
keyStats.demographics: Preterm infants under 32 weeks gestation account for over 40 percent of severe cases; male infants have roughly 1.5x the incidence of females
keyStats.avgAge: Onset within first 48-72 hours of life for early-onset disease; transplacental TORCH pneumonia present at delivery
keyStats.globalCases: Lower respiratory infection contributes to ~13 percent of all neonatal deaths globally (~700,000 per year, WHO 2022)

definition: Congenital pneumonia (ICD-10: P23) is an inflammatory infection of neonatal lung tissue acquired in utero, during the peripartum period, or within the first 48 to 72 hours after birth, distinguishing it from later hospital-acquired neonatal pneumonia. The infection reaches the fetal or newborn lung through three principal routes: hematogenous transplacental spread of TORCH pathogens (cytomegalovirus, rubella, Toxoplasma gondii, Treponema pallidum, Listeria monocytogenes, herpes simplex virus), ascending infection from the maternal genital tract after rupture of membranes (Group B Streptococcus, Escherichia coli, Klebsiella, anaerobes, especially in the setting of chorioamnionitis), and aspiration of infected amniotic fluid or vaginal secretions during delivery. The pulmonary response combines pathogen-driven cytotoxicity, surfactant inactivation, alveolar exudate, and a brisk innate immune cascade that often overlaps clinically and radiographically with respiratory distress syndrome (surfactant deficiency) and transient tachypnea of the newborn. The ICD-10 code P23 subdivides by organism (P23.0 viral, P23.2 staphylococcal, P23.3 Group B Streptococcus, P23.4 E. coli, P23.6 other bacterial, P23.9 unspecified). Management sits with neonatology in level II-IV nurseries, with pediatric pulmonology, infectious disease, and pediatric intensive care joining for complex or refractory cases.

typesClassification.description: Acquired in utero by ascending infection or aspiration of contaminated amniotic fluid during birth. Group B Streptococcus and Escherichia coli account for over 70 percent of culture-positive cases. Presents within the first 24-72 hours of life, frequently as part of early-onset sepsis.

Hematogenous infection across the placenta by TORCH organisms (cytomegalovirus, rubella, Toxoplasma, syphilis, Listeria, herpes simplex). Infants are infected before birth and may show pneumonia alongside hepatosplenomegaly, rash, jaundice, and microcephaly.

typesClassification.type: Late-onset congenital pneumonia (72 hours to 7 days)
typesClassification.description: Develops after the initial perinatal window but is still considered congenital when the infant has not been discharged from delivery care. Pathogen spectrum starts to overlap with hospital-acquired disease (coagulase-negative staphylococci, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, RSV outbreaks).

Driven by inhalation of meconium-stained or infected amniotic fluid at delivery. Bacterial superinfection of chemically injured lung tissue is common and often involves anaerobes from the maternal genital tract.

primarySymptoms: Tachypnea over 60 breaths per minute, present in roughly 90 percent of cases and typically the first sign within the first hour of life.
Audible grunting on expiration as the infant generates auto-PEEP against a partially closed glottis to keep alveoli open.
Intercostal, subcostal, and substernal retractions that worsen over the first 6-12 hours and reflect rising work of breathing.
Nasal flaring during inspiration, easily seen at the bedside and useful as a rapidly trackable sign of respiratory distress.
Central cyanosis around the lips and tongue with pulse oximetry typically dropping below 90 percent on room air.
Temperature instability presenting as hypothermia below 36.5 degrees Celsius more often than fever in preterm infants, and either hypothermia or fever in term newborns.
Poor feeding, weak suck, and lethargy as work of breathing crowds out oral intake within the first 24 hours.
Apneic episodes lasting more than 20 seconds, often with bradycardia, particularly in preterm infants under 34 weeks gestation.
Crackles, rales, or decreased breath sounds on auscultation, sometimes asymmetric when one lung lobe is preferentially involved.
Tachycardia above 160 beats per minute with prolonged capillary refill, signaling early septic shock when present.
earlyWarningSigns: Maternal chorioamnionitis or intrapartum fever above 38 degrees Celsius
Prolonged rupture of membranes (over 18 hours) before delivery
Maternal Group B Streptococcus colonization without complete intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis
Meconium-stained amniotic fluid, especially thick particulate meconium with non-reassuring fetal status
Foul-smelling amniotic fluid or maternal uterine tenderness during labor
emergencySigns: Apnea lasting more than 20 seconds with bradycardia below 100 beats per minute or sustained desaturation below 85 percent
Cyanosis unresponsive to supplemental oxygen, suggesting severe ventilation-perfusion mismatch or persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn
Hypotension with capillary refill over 4 seconds, mottled skin, and lactate above 4 mmol/L, indicating septic shock
Seizure activity or bulging fontanelle suggesting concurrent meningitis (occurs in 5-15 percent of early-onset neonatal sepsis)
Rapidly worsening chest radiograph with whiteout pattern and rising oxygen requirement above FiO2 0.6 within hours

causes.description: The dominant pathogen of early-onset neonatal pneumonia and sepsis in the era before universal intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis, and still a leading cause where prophylaxis is incomplete. Maternal colonization rates of 10-30 percent translate to vertical transmission in roughly 50 percent of exposed infants and invasive disease in 1-2 percent of those colonized.

causes.cause: Escherichia coli and other Gram-negative enteric organisms
causes.description: E. coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Enterobacter cloacae ascend from the maternal genital tract, especially after prolonged rupture of membranes or chorioamnionitis. E. coli has overtaken Group B Streptococcus as the leading cause of early-onset sepsis in very-low-birth-weight infants in many centers since universal GBS prophylaxis.

An intracellular Gram-positive rod acquired through contaminated soft cheese, deli meats, or unpasteurized dairy in pregnancy. Causes a granulomatous transplacental infection with miliary lung lesions and a characteristic salmon-pink rash. Mortality without prompt ampicillin reaches 30 percent.

Cytomegalovirus, rubella, Toxoplasma gondii, Treponema pallidum (congenital syphilis), and herpes simplex virus cross the placenta and seed the fetal lung. Pneumonia often accompanies hepatosplenomegaly, jaundice, microcephaly, intracranial calcifications, and skin findings, prompting full TORCH workup.

causes.cause: Aspiration of infected or meconium-stained amniotic fluid
causes.description: Fetal gasping in response to intrauterine hypoxia or during birth draws contaminated amniotic fluid deep into the airways. Meconium itself inactivates surfactant and predisposes to secondary bacterial pneumonia, most often with anaerobes and Gram-negative enterics.

causes.cause: Anaerobes from the maternal genital tract
causes.description: Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, and peptostreptococci ascend in the setting of chorioamnionitis, especially with prolonged rupture of membranes over 24 hours. They often coexist with aerobic organisms and require coverage with ampicillin plus an aminoglycoside or clindamycin.

When pneumonia onset is within the late congenital window (3-7 days), pathogens shift toward coagulase-negative staphylococci, Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and respiratory viruses including respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) and parainfluenza, especially in extended NICU stays.

riskFactors.description: Preterm infants have immature lung architecture, surfactant deficiency, and lower mucosal immunity. Incidence of congenital pneumonia in infants under 32 weeks is 10-20 times higher than in term infants and mortality reaches 50 percent at extreme prematurity.

Vaginal-rectal GBS colonization in 10-30 percent of pregnant women. Without intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP) the risk of early-onset GBS disease in exposed infants is 1-2 percent; with adequate IAP the rate drops by over 80 percent (CDC GBS guidance 2019).

riskFactors.factor: Prolonged rupture of membranes over 18 hours
riskFactors.description: Each additional hour beyond 18 increases ascending infection risk by approximately 2 percent. Membranes ruptured for more than 24 hours roughly triple the risk of early-onset neonatal sepsis and pneumonia.

Clinical chorioamnionitis (maternal fever plus uterine tenderness, fetal tachycardia, or purulent amniotic fluid) is the strongest single intrapartum predictor, increasing neonatal pneumonia and sepsis risk roughly 4-fold.

riskFactors.factor: Meconium-stained amniotic fluid with fetal distress
riskFactors.description: Thick meconium-stained fluid with non-reassuring fetal heart tracing carries a 5-10 percent incidence of meconium aspiration syndrome, many cases of which develop superimposed bacterial pneumonia within 48 hours.
Male newborns have roughly 1.3-1.5x the incidence of severe early-onset sepsis and pneumonia compared with females, an effect tied to X-linked immune gene dosage that remains incompletely understood.

riskFactors.factor: Maternal urinary tract or genital infection during pregnancy
riskFactors.description: Untreated maternal UTI, bacterial vaginosis, or sexually transmitted infection (chlamydia, gonorrhea, syphilis) raises the risk of ascending or transplacental neonatal infection by 2-4 fold.

Independent of gestational age, low birth weight reflects intrauterine growth restriction or extreme prematurity and is associated with reduced thymic output, impaired neutrophil function, and 3-5 times higher pneumonia mortality.

riskFactors.factor: Invasive mechanical ventilation in the first week
riskFactors.description: Endotracheal intubation bypasses upper airway defenses and seeds the lung with skin and oropharyngeal flora. Each additional day of ventilation increases ventilator-associated pneumonia risk by approximately 3 percent in NICU populations.

riskFactors.factor: Lack of antenatal corticosteroids in preterm delivery
riskFactors.description: Antenatal betamethasone or dexamethasone in pregnancies under 34 weeks reduces neonatal respiratory distress syndrome by roughly 30 percent and indirectly reduces secondary pneumonia by lowering ventilator days (Cochrane 2020).

affectedDemographics: Preterm infants account for ~40 percent of all congenital pneumonia cases and over 60 percent of deaths
Low- and middle-income countries report 5-10 times the case-fatality rate of high-income settings, driven by limited surfactant access and mechanical ventilation
Male infants outnumber female infants in early-onset disease by roughly 1.5 to 1
Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia together account for over 80 percent of global neonatal pneumonia deaths (WHO 2022)
Indigenous populations in high-income countries (including Indigenous Australian, New Zealand Maori, and Native American infants) show 2-3 times higher early-onset sepsis and pneumonia incidence
diagnosisOverview: Congenital pneumonia is a clinical-radiographic diagnosis supported by microbiology and inflammatory markers rather than a single confirmatory test. The clinician integrates maternal history (GBS status, duration of rupture of membranes, chorioamnionitis, fever, maternal infections), delivery events (meconium-stained fluid, Apgar scores, resuscitation), and the infant's respiratory pattern within the first hours of life. Pulse oximetry, blood gas analysis, and bedside examination identify and grade respiratory distress, while a chest radiograph distinguishes between common neonatal mimics: a granular ground-glass pattern with air bronchograms suggests surfactant deficiency (RDS), streaky perihilar markings with fluid in the fissures suggest transient tachypnea, patchy or coalescent infiltrates with consolidation favor pneumonia, and coarse asymmetric infiltrates with hyperinflation suggest meconium aspiration. Blood culture is drawn before antibiotics whenever feasible. A complete blood count with differential is interpreted using the immature-to-total (I:T) neutrophil ratio over 0.2 and absolute neutrophil count nomograms; C-reactive protein and procalcitonin trend over 24-48 hours add specificity. Tracheal aspirate for Gram stain and culture is informative if the infant is intubated in the first few hours. Lumbar puncture is performed when meningitis is suspected (focal neurology, seizures, persistent positive blood culture). TORCH workup is added when transplacental infection is suspected, with maternal serology, infant CMV urine PCR, rapid plasma reagin for syphilis, HSV PCR on blood and CSF, and Listeria culture when there is a compatible maternal exposure. Empiric antibiotics are started without waiting for results.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The single most useful imaging study. Differentiates patterns suggestive of pneumonia (patchy or coalescent infiltrates, consolidation, effusion) from RDS (diffuse ground-glass with air bronchograms), transient tachypnea (perihilar streaking, fluid in fissures), and meconium aspiration (coarse asymmetric infiltrates, hyperinflation).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A bedside portable X-ray performed in the nursery, taking less than five minutes. The infant is positioned supine and a single anteroposterior view is taken; lateral views are added selectively. Radiation dose is very low.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms bacteremic pneumonia and identifies the pathogen for targeted therapy. Positive in roughly 5-10 percent of clinically diagnosed early-onset sepsis or pneumonia, with higher yield when drawn before antibiotics.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 1-2 mL blood sample drawn from a peripheral vein under sterile technique. Preliminary results in 24-48 hours; final identification and sensitivities in 48-72 hours. Repeat cultures are drawn if clinical course worsens.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for leukocytosis, leukopenia (more specific for severe sepsis in neonates), thrombocytopenia, and an immature-to-total neutrophil ratio above 0.2. A normal CBC does not rule out infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small blood sample (0.5-1 mL) drawn alongside the blood culture. Results return within an hour. Interpreted using age-specific neonatal nomograms rather than adult ranges.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Inflammatory markers that rise within 6-12 hours of infection. Serial CRP at 0, 24, and 48 hours is more useful than a single value; persistent normal values support shorter antibiotic courses (Cochrane 2019).

diagnosticTests.test: Tracheal aspirate Gram stain and culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Useful in intubated infants when performed within the first 8 hours of life. Polymorphonuclear cells with intracellular organisms support bacterial pneumonia; later samples are confounded by airway colonization.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small sterile catheter is passed through the endotracheal tube and a sample of secretions is suctioned. Takes a minute and is done at the bedside.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies hypoxemia, hypercapnia, and metabolic acidosis. Guides escalation of respiratory support and identifies persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn when pre-ductal and post-ductal saturations differ by more than 10 percent.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A heel-prick capillary sample or a small arterial puncture, with results in 5-10 minutes from a bedside analyzer.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Performed when meningitis is suspected, blood cultures grow a pathogen, or clinical course is unusual. Concomitant meningitis occurs in 5-15 percent of early-onset neonatal sepsis, including pneumonia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small needle is placed in the lower spinal canal under sterile technique with local anesthetic; 1-2 mL of cerebrospinal fluid is collected. The procedure takes 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Targeted when transplacental infection is suspected by history (maternal serology, exposures) or physical exam (rash, hepatosplenomegaly, microcephaly). Includes CMV urine PCR, HSV PCR, syphilis RPR, toxoplasma IgM, and Listeria culture.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood, urine, and occasionally cerebrospinal fluid samples sent to a reference laboratory. Results typically return within 3-7 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN), congenital heart disease that can mimic pneumonia, and ventricular function in shock. Indicated when oxygen requirement is disproportionate to radiographic findings.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A bedside ultrasound of the heart taking 15-20 minutes, painless and radiation-free.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of congenital pneumonia rests on three pillars started in parallel within the first hour of recognized illness: empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics, respiratory support tailored to the severity of distress, and supportive care of temperature, glucose, hemodynamics, and nutrition. The standard empiric regimen for suspected early-onset disease is intravenous ampicillin plus gentamicin, which together cover the dominant pathogens Group B Streptococcus, Listeria monocytogenes, E. coli, and most other Gram-negative enterics. Ampicillin is dosed 50-100 mg/kg every 8-12 hours depending on gestational age and renal function; gentamicin is dosed using extended-interval neonatal protocols (4-5 mg/kg every 24-48 hours) with trough monitoring after 48 hours. The regimen is narrowed once cultures and sensitivities return, typically by 48-72 hours, and total duration is usually 7-10 days for uncomplicated pneumonia, 14 days when bacteremic with a Gram-negative organism, and 21 days or more for meningitis. Suspected HSV pneumonitis or systemic herpes is treated with high-dose intravenous acyclovir (60 mg/kg/day in three divided doses for 14-21 days). Respiratory support is escalated stepwise: humidified high-flow nasal cannula or nasal CPAP for moderate distress, surfactant administration (poractant alfa or beractant 100-200 mg/kg) when there is surfactant inactivation pattern or coexisting RDS, conventional or high-frequency mechanical ventilation for impending respiratory failure, inhaled nitric oxide for persistent pulmonary hypertension, and extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) for refractory respiratory failure in infants over 34 weeks gestation and 2 kg. Surfactant therapy is used selectively in congenital pneumonia because the protein component is consumed by bacterial proteases; repeat dosing may be needed. Adjunctive care includes thermoregulation in a servo-controlled incubator, careful fluid balance (60-80 mL/kg/day initially, advancing daily), early enteral feeds when stable, and parental presence and skin-to-skin care once respiratory status allows. Treat-to-target principles include normalizing oxygen saturation (90-95 percent for preterm, 95-100 percent for term), pH (7.30-7.45), PCO2 (45-55 mmHg permissive in chronic lung disease prevention), and inflammatory markers (declining CRP, normalizing white count).

medicalTreatments.name: Ampicillin plus gentamicin (empiric first-line)
medicalTreatments.description: Ampicillin 50-100 mg/kg IV every 8-12 hours covers Group B Streptococcus, Listeria, and most Streptococcus and Enterococcus species. Gentamicin 4-5 mg/kg IV every 24-48 hours (extended-interval neonatal dosing) covers E. coli, Klebsiella, and most Enterobacteriaceae. Standard empiric regimen recommended by AAP Red Book and most national neonatal guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Empiric regimen covers over 90 percent of community-onset neonatal bacterial pathogens; survival in term infants with adequate respiratory support reaches 95 percent.

medicalTreatments.name: Cefotaxime or ceftazidime (for suspected meningitis or resistant Gram-negatives)
medicalTreatments.description: Substituted for gentamicin when meningitis is suspected (better CSF penetration) or in late-onset/hospital-acquired pneumonia with concern for resistant Gram-negative organisms. Cefotaxime 50 mg/kg IV every 8-12 hours; ceftazidime 30-50 mg/kg every 8-12 hours when Pseudomonas is suspected.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves therapeutic CSF concentrations in over 80 percent of dosed infants and is the standard adjunct when meningitis coexists.

medicalTreatments.name: Vancomycin (for suspected staphylococcal or hospital-acquired disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Added when methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus or coagulase-negative staphylococci are suspected, typically in late-onset disease, central-line-associated infection, or infants with skin breakdown. Dose 10-15 mg/kg IV every 8-18 hours with trough levels.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clears bacteremia in 70-85 percent within 48-72 hours; longer durations needed for endocarditis or device infection.

medicalTreatments.name: Acyclovir (for suspected or confirmed neonatal HSV)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose IV acyclovir 60 mg/kg/day in three divided doses for 14 days for skin-eye-mouth disease and 21 days for disseminated or CNS disease (NEJM 2011 neonatal HSV trial). Started empirically when HSV is suspected based on maternal lesions, vesicular rash, seizures, or hepatitis pattern.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality of disseminated neonatal HSV from over 80 percent to roughly 30 percent and prevents progression in 90 percent of skin-eye-mouth disease when started early.

medicalTreatments.name: Exogenous surfactant (poractant alfa or beractant 100-200 mg/kg)
medicalTreatments.description: Administered intratracheally when chest radiograph and clinical pattern suggest surfactant inactivation by bacterial pneumonia, meconium, or coexisting RDS. Often given once with re-dosing in 6-12 hours if oxygen requirement remains high. Surfactant is consumed by bacterial proteases, so response in pneumonia is more variable than in pure RDS.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces FiO2 requirement by 20-40 percent within 1-2 hours in responsive infants; Cochrane 2014 review showed reduced mortality and air leak in severe meconium aspiration.

medicalTreatments.name: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) and high-flow nasal cannula
medicalTreatments.description: Non-invasive respiratory support with PEEP 5-8 cm H2O via nasal prongs or mask. First-line for infants with moderate distress and adequate respiratory drive; reduces need for intubation by approximately 30 percent compared with early intubation strategies (COIN, SUPPORT trials).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Avoids intubation in 60-70 percent of preterm infants with moderate distress, lowering bronchopulmonary dysplasia risk.

medicalTreatments.name: Mechanical ventilation (conventional or high-frequency oscillatory)
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated for impending respiratory failure (pH below 7.25, PCO2 above 60, FiO2 above 0.6 to maintain saturations, or apnea unresponsive to caffeine and CPAP). High-frequency oscillatory ventilation is preferred for severe air leak or refractory hypoxemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Survival in ventilated term infants with congenital pneumonia is 85-95 percent; in extreme preterm infants under 28 weeks, 60-75 percent.

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled nitric oxide (iNO) at 20 ppm
medicalTreatments.description: Selective pulmonary vasodilator used for persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN) complicating congenital pneumonia in infants over 34 weeks gestation. Reduces oxygenation index and need for ECMO by roughly 40 percent (NINOS trial 1997).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves oxygenation in 60-70 percent of term infants with PPHN within 30 minutes; reduces ECMO need without changing overall mortality.

medicalTreatments.name: Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for refractory respiratory failure in infants over 34 weeks gestation and over 2 kg with reversible disease (oxygenation index above 40 or persistent oxygenation index above 25 despite optimized therapy). Provides cardiopulmonary support for 5-14 days while lungs heal.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Survival 60-80 percent in selected term infants with severe congenital pneumonia or meconium aspiration syndrome (ELSO Registry 2023).

surgicalOptions.name: Chest tube drainage for empyema or pneumothorax
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for moderate-to-large empyema, complicated parapneumonic effusion, or air leak (pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum) compromising ventilation. A small-bore pigtail catheter or larger thoracostomy tube is placed under local anesthetic.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolves air leak in over 90 percent of cases; empyema clearance in 70-85 percent with chest tube alone, with fibrinolytic instillation reserved for loculated collections.

surgicalOptions.name: Tracheostomy
surgicalOptions.description: Considered rarely, for infants with prolonged ventilator dependence (over 6-8 weeks) and irreversible airway pathology. Most congenital pneumonia is reversible and avoids tracheostomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: When required for chronic lung disease, supports home ventilation and discharge in 70-80 percent of selected infants.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotic supplementation (Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium strains)
alternativeTreatments.description: Cochrane 2023 review of preterm infant probiotics shows reduced necrotizing enterocolitis and a modest 20 percent reduction in late-onset sepsis including secondary pneumonia. Adjunctive only and used under neonatal team guidance.

alternativeTreatments.name: Kangaroo mother care (skin-to-skin contact)
alternativeTreatments.description: Sustained skin-to-skin care between parent and infant lowers infection rate, improves thermoregulation, and reduces mortality in preterm infants by roughly 25 percent in low-resource settings (WHO iKMC trial 2021). Used as soon as the infant is hemodynamically stable.

whySeeSpecialist: Any newborn with persistent tachypnea, grunting, retractions, or oxygen requirement beyond the first hour of life requires evaluation by a neonatology team in a hospital with level II or higher nursery capability. Transfer to a level III or IV neonatal intensive care unit is indicated for infants needing mechanical ventilation, surfactant therapy, inhaled nitric oxide, ECMO, or for preterm infants under 32 weeks. Pediatric infectious disease consultation is added for unusual pathogens, TORCH workup, treatment failure, or complex antibiotic regimens. Pediatric pulmonology follow-up is recommended after discharge for any infant who required mechanical ventilation longer than 7 days or developed chronic lung disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a delivery hospital with at least a level II nursery if any prenatal risk factor for neonatal infection is present (preterm labor, GBS positivity, prolonged rupture of membranes, chorioamnionitis). Verify the unit's GBS prophylaxis protocols and antibiotic stewardship practices, the availability of 24-hour neonatology and respiratory therapy coverage, and access to surfactant, inhaled nitric oxide, and high-frequency ventilation. For preterm or complex pregnancies, plan delivery at a regional perinatal center with level III or IV capability and on-site ECMO if available. After discharge, continuity with a developmental follow-up clinic improves long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes.
hospitalCriteria: Level II nursery or higher with 24-hour neonatology and respiratory therapy coverage
Capacity for blood gas analysis, portable chest radiography, and microbiology within the hospital
Stock of surfactant, ampicillin, gentamicin, cefotaxime, acyclovir, and inhaled nitric oxide on-site or rapidly available
Access to mechanical ventilation including high-frequency oscillation
Established transfer pathway to a level III or IV neonatal intensive care unit when escalation is needed
keyFacilities: Tertiary academic neonatal intensive care units with on-site ECMO programs
Regional perinatal referral centers with level III NICU capability
Children's hospitals with pediatric infectious disease and pulmonology services for complex or refractory cases
preventionStrategies: Universal antenatal screening for Group B Streptococcus at 36-37 weeks gestation and intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (penicillin G or ampicillin) for colonized women or those with risk factors (CDC GBS guidance 2019)
Timely diagnosis and treatment of maternal urinary tract and genital infections during pregnancy, including syphilis, chlamydia, gonorrhea, and bacterial vaginosis

Antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone or dexamethasone) for pregnancies between 24 and 34 weeks at risk of preterm delivery, reducing RDS and indirectly lowering secondary pneumonia (Cochrane 2020)
Avoidance of unpasteurized dairy, soft cheese, and deli meats in pregnancy to prevent maternal Listeria infection
Early management of preterm prelabour rupture of membranes with latency antibiotics and timed delivery when chorioamnionitis develops
Routine maternal immunization including influenza vaccine and Tdap (pertussis) in every pregnancy, plus the new maternal RSV vaccine where licensed
lifestyleModifications: Exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months, which lowers the risk of late-onset sepsis and lower respiratory infection by roughly 50 percent
Strict hand hygiene by all caregivers before any contact with the infant during the first 6 weeks of life
Limit visitors during the first 6 weeks and avoid contact with people with active respiratory infection
Maintain a smoke-free home environment from pregnancy onward, as prenatal and postnatal tobacco exposure raises lower respiratory infection rates by 40-70 percent
Ensure the infant receives all routine vaccines on schedule including pneumococcal conjugate, hepatitis B, and rotavirus
Caregivers and household contacts should receive annual influenza vaccine, Tdap, and (where licensed) RSV vaccination to cocoon the newborn

recommended: Early initiation of expressed breast milk via gavage feeds in stable infants, advancing as tolerated to direct breastfeeding
Donor pasteurized human milk when mother's own milk is unavailable for preterm or sick infants
Fortified human milk for preterm infants to support growth without increasing infection risk
Vitamin D supplementation 400 IU daily for breastfed infants per AAP recommendation
Iron supplementation starting at 4 months for breastfed term infants and earlier for preterm infants
avoid: Cow's milk-based formula prepared with non-sterile water in low-resource settings without proper sterilization
Honey and home-prepared cereals in the first 12 months
Delayed initiation of feeds beyond what is clinically necessary, which prolongs central venous catheter use and raises late-onset sepsis risk
Unpasteurized milk products at any age

exerciseGuidelines: Not applicable for newborns. Developmental positioning, supervised tummy time once respiratory status is stable, and early physiotherapy for preterm infants support neuromuscular development without affecting pneumonia recovery. Discharge planning includes regular pediatric follow-up at 1, 2, 4, and 6 months for growth, developmental milestones, and lung function review.
dailyManagement: Monitor respiratory rate, work of breathing, and oxygen saturation at every feed during hospitalization and for the first week after discharge
Complete the full prescribed antibiotic course intravenously; outpatient oral conversion is appropriate only in selected, stable infants under specialist guidance
Track temperature twice daily for the first 2 weeks after discharge and seek urgent review for any temperature below 36 degrees Celsius or above 38 degrees Celsius
Maintain breastfeeding or expressed breast milk feeds and watch for adequate weight gain (20-30 g/day for term, 15-25 g/day for preterm infants)
Attend all follow-up appointments including hearing screen, ophthalmology for preterm infants, and developmental assessments
Keep written records of the pneumonia episode, organism if identified, antibiotic used, and discharge summary for use at future visits
prognosis: For term infants with prompt diagnosis and effective antibiotic and respiratory support, survival exceeds 95 percent with complete recovery of lung function in over 90 percent. Outcomes worsen sharply with prematurity: term infants face roughly 5 percent mortality, late preterm 10 percent, very preterm under 32 weeks 20-30 percent, and extremely preterm under 28 weeks 30-50 percent. Long-term complications include bronchopulmonary dysplasia (chronic lung disease in 20-40 percent of ventilated preterm infants), recurrent wheezing in early childhood, mildly reduced lung function on spirometry into adolescence, and an increased rate of asthma diagnosis by school age. Neurodevelopmental outcomes are tied less to the pneumonia itself than to gestational age, hypoxic episodes, concurrent meningitis, and prolonged ventilation; close developmental follow-up to age 2 is recommended for any preterm infant with congenital pneumonia. Recurrence of congenital pneumonia after the neonatal period is rare; recurrent lower respiratory infections in infancy should prompt evaluation for immunodeficiency, congenital pulmonary airway malformation, or aspiration.
recoveryTimeline: Acute respiratory distress typically peaks at 24-72 hours and improves over 3-7 days with appropriate antibiotics and respiratory support. Most term infants are weaned off supplemental oxygen by day 5-7 and discharged within 7-14 days. Preterm infants follow a longer trajectory: extubation to CPAP within 7-14 days, weaning to room air by 3-6 weeks corrected age, and discharge typically at 36-40 weeks postmenstrual age. Antibiotic courses are 7-10 days for uncomplicated pneumonia, 14 days for bacteremic Gram-negative disease, and 21 days or more for meningitis. Pediatric pulmonology follow-up at 3, 6, and 12 months screens for chronic lung disease and growth.
complications: Septic shock with hypotension and multi-organ dysfunction, occurring in 10-20 percent of culture-positive cases and carrying mortality above 30 percent
Concurrent meningitis (5-15 percent of early-onset sepsis), with risk of seizures, hydrocephalus, and long-term neurodevelopmental delay
Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN), particularly with sepsis or meconium aspiration, raising mortality 3-5 fold without inhaled nitric oxide
Air leak syndromes (pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum, pulmonary interstitial emphysema) in mechanically ventilated infants
Bronchopulmonary dysplasia (chronic neonatal lung disease) in 20-40 percent of ventilated preterm infants, with long-term reactive airway disease
Empyema or lung abscess from delayed treatment or resistant organisms, occasionally requiring chest tube or surgical drainage
Hearing loss from aminoglycoside exposure or sensorineural injury after sepsis, identified through universal newborn hearing screen

supportResources.name: American Academy of Pediatrics — HealthyChildren.org Newborn Care
supportResources.description: AAP-curated patient information on newborn health, infection, and feeding.

supportResources.name: CDC Group B Strep (GBS) Prevention
supportResources.description: Public health guidance for parents and clinicians on GBS screening and intrapartum prevention of early-onset neonatal disease.

supportResources.name: March of Dimes — NICU Family Support
supportResources.description: Patient organization with resources for families with infants in the NICU, including infection and prematurity content.

confusedWithConditions.name: Community-acquired pneumonia (older infants and children)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Community-acquired pneumonia presents beyond the first month of life with fever, cough, and focal consolidation. Congenital pneumonia presents within 48-72 hours of birth, frequently without classic cough, and is driven by perinatal pathogens such as Group B Streptococcus, E. coli, and Listeria rather than the Streptococcus pneumoniae and Mycoplasma that dominate later infancy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Congenital Syphilis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Congenital syphilis is a transplacental TORCH infection that can include pneumonia alba but is dominated by hepatosplenomegaly, snuffles, mucocutaneous rash, periostitis on bone X-rays, and positive maternal and infant RPR. Congenital pneumonia from bacterial or non-syphilitic causes lacks these systemic findings.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neonatal Escherichia coli Sepsis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Neonatal E. coli sepsis is a systemic bacterial bloodstream infection in newborns that often includes pneumonia but can present primarily as meningitis, urinary tract infection, or septic shock. Congenital pneumonia is the lung-localized phenotype of similar perinatal pathogen exposure; both conditions overlap and are treated with the same empiric ampicillin plus gentamicin regimen.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the pathophysiologic mechanism of lung infection; understanding adult and pediatric pneumonia clarifies the neonatal-specific pathogens and management of congenital pneumonia.

relatedConditions.name: Congenital Syphilis
relatedConditions.relevance: TORCH transplacental infection that frequently includes pneumonia alba as one of its pulmonary manifestations and is part of the same diagnostic workup.

relatedConditions.name: Escherichia coli Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: E. coli is now the leading cause of early-onset sepsis and pneumonia in very-low-birth-weight infants since universal GBS intrapartum prophylaxis.

relatedConditions.name: Listerial Meningitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Listeria monocytogenes causes transplacental neonatal infection with pneumonia and meningitis; recognition of one prompts evaluation for the other.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes Simplex Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Neonatal herpes simplex virus infection includes pneumonitis as a feature of disseminated disease and requires empiric high-dose acyclovir alongside antibacterial therapy.

faqs.question: What is congenital pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Congenital pneumonia is a lung infection in a newborn acquired before delivery, during birth, or within the first 48 to 72 hours of life. It presents with tachypnea, grunting, retractions, and oxygen requirement, and is most often caused by Group B Streptococcus, E. coli, or TORCH organisms.

faqs.question: How common is congenital pneumonia?
faqs.answer: It complicates roughly 1 to 10 of every 1,000 live births in high-income settings and 5 to 50 per 1,000 in low- and middle-income countries. Risk rises with prematurity, prolonged rupture of membranes, and maternal chorioamnionitis.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of pneumonia in a newborn?
faqs.answer: The earliest signs are fast breathing above 60 per minute, grunting, chest wall retractions, nasal flaring, and bluish lips. Temperature instability, poor feeding, and lethargy follow. Signs typically appear within the first hours of life.

faqs.question: What causes congenital pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Three routes account for most cases: transplacental TORCH infection, ascending infection from the maternal genital tract after rupture of membranes, and aspiration of infected amniotic fluid at birth. Group B Streptococcus and E. coli together explain over 70 percent of culture-positive disease.

faqs.question: How is congenital pneumonia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines maternal history, respiratory distress signs, a chest X-ray showing patchy infiltrates, blood culture, complete blood count, and CRP or procalcitonin. Tracheal aspirate culture, lumbar puncture, and TORCH panel are added when clinically indicated.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for congenital pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Empiric IV ampicillin plus gentamicin is started within the first hour and continued 7 to 10 days, narrowed once cultures return. Acyclovir is added if herpes is suspected. Respiratory support escalates from oxygen and CPAP to mechanical ventilation and, rarely, ECMO.

faqs.question: How serious is pneumonia in a newborn?
faqs.answer: Term infants with prompt treatment reach over 95 percent survival with full recovery. Preterm mortality runs 10 percent late preterm, 20 to 30 percent very preterm, and up to 50 percent in extremely preterm infants. Early recognition is the single biggest driver of outcome.

faqs.question: How is congenital pneumonia different from respiratory distress syndrome?
faqs.answer: RDS is caused by surfactant deficiency in preterm infants and shows a diffuse ground-glass X-ray with air bronchograms. Congenital pneumonia is an infection producing patchy infiltrates with positive inflammatory markers or cultures. They often coexist.

faqs.question: Can a baby get pneumonia from the mother during birth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vertical transmission is a major route. The infant can acquire infection by aspirating contaminated amniotic fluid, direct contact with the maternal genital tract, or ascending bacteria after rupture of membranes. Group B Streptococcus, E. coli, and herpes are the leading pathogens.

faqs.question: Why is Group B Strep important in newborn pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Group B Streptococcus colonizes 10 to 30 percent of pregnant women, and exposed infants have a 1 to 2 percent risk of invasive disease without prophylaxis. Intrapartum penicillin to colonized women cuts early-onset GBS disease by over 80 percent (CDC, 2019).

faqs.question: Can congenital pneumonia be prevented?
faqs.answer: Many cases are preventable. Antenatal GBS screening with intrapartum prophylaxis, treatment of maternal genital and urinary infections, antenatal corticosteroids in preterm labor, and avoidance of unpasteurized dairy reduce risk. Maternal flu, pertussis, and RSV vaccines add further protection.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for congenital pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Survival depends on gestational age. Term infants reach over 95 percent. Late preterm is about 90 percent, very preterm 70 to 80 percent, and extremely preterm 50 to 70 percent. Septic shock, pulmonary hypertension, and meningitis predict worse outcomes.

faqs.question: How long do babies stay in hospital for congenital pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Term infants with uncomplicated disease are discharged within 7 to 14 days after IV antibiotics finish. Preterm infants stay until 36 to 40 weeks postmenstrual age. Complex cases needing ventilation, surfactant, or ECMO may stay 4 to 12 weeks.

faqs.question: What are the long-term effects of congenital pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Most term infants recover fully. Preterm infants who needed prolonged ventilation may develop bronchopulmonary dysplasia in 20 to 40 percent, recurrent wheezing, and a higher rate of asthma by school age. Long-term risk depends on gestational age and any meningitis.

faqs.question: Is congenital pneumonia contagious to family members?
faqs.answer: The pneumonia itself is not directly contagious, but the maternal source (GBS, herpes, syphilis, Listeria) may need evaluation or treatment of the mother. Hospital-acquired pathogens such as RSV during NICU outbreaks can spread between infants.

faqs.question: Can a baby get congenital pneumonia if the mother had no symptoms?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most maternal GBS colonization, urinary tract infection, and bacterial vaginosis are asymptomatic yet still pose risk to the newborn. This is why routine antenatal screening exists and why preterm or unwell newborns are evaluated for infection regardless of maternal symptoms.

faqs.question: How does congenital pneumonia differ from meconium aspiration?
faqs.answer: Meconium aspiration is chemical lung injury from inhaled meconium-stained fluid. Congenital pneumonia is infectious. They often overlap because meconium-stained fluid can be contaminated, and bacterial superinfection of injured lung tissue is common.

faqs.question: Will my baby need surfactant for congenital pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Surfactant is given selectively when the chest X-ray and clinical pattern suggest surfactant inactivation by infection, meconium, or coexisting RDS. Response is more variable than in pure RDS because bacterial proteases consume surfactant, and re-dosing in 6 to 12 hours is sometimes needed.

faqs.question: Does breastfeeding protect against pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months cuts late-onset sepsis and lower respiratory infection by roughly 50 percent through secretory IgA, lactoferrin, and other immune factors. Expressed breast milk is started by gavage as soon as the infant is stable.

faqs.question: What follow-up does my baby need after congenital pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Follow-up includes newborn hearing screen, pediatric review at 1, 2, 4, and 6 months for growth and milestones, pediatric pulmonology at 3, 6, and 12 months for prolonged-ventilation infants, and developmental clinic to age 2 for preterm infants. Vaccinations follow the standard schedule.

faqs.question: Can congenital pneumonia come back later in infancy?
faqs.answer: Recurrence after the neonatal period is rare. New infections are typically community-acquired and viral. Recurrent pneumonia in the same lobe should prompt evaluation for immunodeficiency, congenital airway malformation, reflux with aspiration, or anatomic airway abnormality.

faqs.question: When should I worry about a newborn's breathing at home?
faqs.answer: Seek urgent care if a newborn shows fast breathing over 60 per minute, grunting, chest retractions, blue or grey lips, temperature below 36 or above 38 degrees Celsius, poor feeding over 2 to 3 feeds, or unusual lethargy. Apneic pauses over 20 seconds need emergency care.

simpleName: Congenital pneumonia (newborn lung infection present at birth or within 72 hours)
metaDescription: Congenital pneumonia: newborn lung infection at birth or within 72 hours. Causes, signs, diagnosis, and treatment reviewed by our neonatology medical board.
